questions.questionText: A 70-year-old woman falls and presents with shortened, externally rotated left lower limb. X-ray shows a subcapital femoral neck fracture with complete displacement (Garden IV). Best management?
questions.optionB: Hemiarthroplasty (replacement of femoral head — preferred for displaced (Garden III/IV) femoral neck fractures in elderly patients; provides faster recovery and mobilization)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Displaced femoral neck fracture in elderly = hemiarthroplasty (not internal fixation). Why correct: Garden III/IV (displaced): high risk of AVN and nonunion with fixation → hemiarthroplasty in elderly (>60-65 years). Young patients → internal fixation (cancellous screws) regardless of displacement (preserve native head). Why A wrong: Conservative — high morbidity/mortality (bedsores, DVT, pneumonia). Why C wrong: DHS — for intertrochanteric fractures (not neck fractures). Why D wrong: PFN — for intertrochanteric/subtrochanteric fractures. Clinical relevance: Garden classification: I (incomplete/valgus impacted), II (complete undisplaced), III (partially displaced), IV (completely displaced). Femoral neck fractures are INTRAcapsular → retrograde blood supply at risk → AVN. Exam trap: Intracapsular (neck) = AVN risk → hemiarthroplasty if displaced/elderly. Extracapsular (intertrochanteric) = NO AVN → internal fixation (DHS). Memory aid: 'Displaced Neck in Old = arthroplasty (Don't fix, replace).' Reference: Maheshwari – Lower Limb Fractures.
questions.conceptTag: Femoral neck Garden hemiarthroplasty elderly AVN

questions.questionText: Tibial shaft fractures have a high rate of delayed union and non-union primarily because:
questions.optionB: The subcutaneous anteromedial surface has poor soft tissue coverage and blood supply (tibia has limited periosteal blood supply)
questions.optionD: The fracture always involves the joint
questions.explanation: Core concept: Tibia is subcutaneous → poor soft tissue coverage → compromised blood supply → healing challenges. Why correct: Anteromedial surface of tibia is immediately subcutaneous (no muscle covering) → limited periosteal blood supply → higher rates of: delayed union, nonunion, infection, compound fractures (skin easily breached). Why A wrong: Can be low-energy too (stress fractures in runners). Why C wrong: Many tibial shaft fractures treated conservatively (Sarmiento brace). Why D wrong: Shaft fractures don't involve joint. Clinical relevance: Most common long bone to develop compound (open) fracture. Gustilo-Anderson classification for open fractures. Intramedullary nail = gold standard fixation for diaphyseal tibial fractures. Exam trap: Tibia shaft fracture is the most common long bone to have delayed union/nonunion AND most common to be compound. Memory aid: 'Tibia = Thin skin covering = Trouble healing.' Reference: Maheshwari – Tibial Fractures.
questions.conceptTag: Tibial shaft subcutaneous nonunion compound open
